original_hypothesis.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Permanent DoS via Arithmetic Underflow
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn(), the calculation `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` will underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8+) if the contract's TITN balance at snapshot time is less than totalTitnClaimable. This occurs when TGT deposits exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (ECON-H001), causing totalTitnClaimable > actual TITN balance. The snapshot is attempted on every call when remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0 (which it stays at if balance < totalTitnClaimable), permanently DoS-ing all post-360-day claims.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a cascading consequence of ECON-H001 that permanently locks all remaining user funds after 360 days. Since claimTitn is blocked after 360 days (>= check), and withdrawRemainingTitn always reverts, users have NO claim path. This creates a dead-end state machine with no recovery.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line ~117: `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` — no check that remainingTitnAfter1Year >= initialTotalClaimable. If contract balance < totalTitnClaimable at snapshot, underflow reverts permanently.

preconditions: Over-allocation occurred (ECON-H001) OR owner withdrew TITN via withdraw()
At least one user has claimableTitnPerUser > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable with no underflow guard
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Over-deposit of TGT makes contract balance < totalTitnClaimable at 360-day mark
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not a profit attack but permanent fund lock: all unclaimed TITN becomes irrecoverable

economic_questions: Under what over-allocation thresholds does the underflow occur?
If some users claimed before 360d, does partial claiming prevent the underflow?
Is there any recovery path once the underflow condition is set?
validation_requirements: Simulate: deposit > 579M TGT, advance to 360d, call withdrawRemainingTitn and verify revert
Verify re-initialization loop: remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0, every call re-enters init and underflows
Confirm no admin escape hatch (no function to manually set snapshot values)

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent once 360-day mark is reached with insolvent state

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Condition is latent; manifests automatically at 360-day mark
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All unclaimed TITN permanently locked; users who delayed claiming lose everything

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who did not claim via claimTitn before 360 days
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of claimable TITN plus proportional bonus
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: totalTitnClaimable at 360-day mark (could be majority of TITN_ARB)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Track totalTitnClaimable vs titn.balanceOf(contract) over time
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm: if balanceOf < totalTitnClaimable, subtraction reverts; and remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0 causing perpetual re-initialization

validation_result.mechanism: Arithmetic underflow causing permanent DoS in withdrawRemainingTitn
validation_result.impact_summary: If the contract's TITN balance at the 360-day snapshot is less than totalTitnClaimable (due to over-allocation or owner withdrawal), the subtraction `remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` underflows and reverts. Because the revert rolls back the snapshot state, every subsequent call re-enters initialization and underflows again, permanently locking all post-360-day claims.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `withdrawRemainingTitn()` function at MergeTgt.sol:100-132 initializes `remainingTitnAfter1Year` and `initialTotalClaimable` on the first call after 360 days. It then computes `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` (line ~122). In Solidity 0.8+, if `remainingTitnAfter1Year < initialTotalClaimable`, this reverts due to arithmetic underflow. Critically, since the revert rolls back ALL state changes in the transaction—including the initialization of `remainingTitnAfter1Year` and `initialTotalClaimable`—the snapshot is never persisted. On the next call, `remainingTitnAfter1Year` is still 0, the initialization block re-executes with the same (or worse) values, and the same underflow occurs. This creates a permanent DoS loop.

Two paths lead to this condition:
1. **Over-allocation (no TGT deposit cap):** `onTokenTransfer` accepts unlimited TGT. `quoteTitn` computes `titnAmount = (tgtAmount * TITN_ARB) / TGT_TO_EXCHANGE` per deposit with no cumulative cap. If aggregate TGT deposited > TGT_TO_EXCHANGE, then totalTitnClaimable > TITN_ARB. Since the contract holds exactly TITN_ARB of TITN, `balance < totalTitnClaimable`.
2. **Owner withdrawal:** `withdraw(titn, amount)` allows the owner to remove TITN at any time, reducing balance without reducing totalTitnClaimable.

In either case, at 360 days: `remainingTitnAfter1Year = titn.balanceOf(this) < totalTitnClaimable = initialTotalClaimable`, and the subtraction underflows. Since `claimTitn` is blocked after 360 days (`>= 360 days` check at line ~98), users have NO viable claim path.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Line 122 of MergeTgt.sol performs `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable;` with no preceding check that `remainingTitnAfter1Year >= initialTotalClaimable`. The initialization at lines 110-115 sets `remainingTitnAfter1Year = titn.balanceOf(address(this))` and `initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable`. There is no enforcement anywhere in the contract that `titn.balanceOf(address(this)) >= totalTitnClaimable`. The `onTokenTransfer` function (lines 68-84) increments `totalTitnClaimable` without any cap, and `withdraw` (line 58) decrements balance without decrementing totalTitnClaimable. Both paths can cause `balance < totalTitnClaimable`.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Line 122: `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable;` underflows if the contract's TITN balance (snapshotted into remainingTitnAfter1Year) is less than totalTitnClaimable (snapshotted into initialTotalClaimable). Since the revert rolls back the snapshot initialization (lines 110-115), remainingTitnAfter1Year remains 0, causing every subsequent call to re-attempt initialization and hit the same underflow. This permanently DoS-es all post-360-day claims.

No cumulative cap on totalTitnClaimable. Each deposit independently computes titnOut via quoteTitn without checking if cumulative allocation exceeds TITN_ARB. If total TGT deposited > TGT_TO_EXCHANGE, totalTitnClaimable > TITN_ARB, triggering the underflow condition in withdrawRemainingTitn.
Owner can withdraw TITN without any restriction, reducing the contract's TITN balance below totalTitnClaimable. This directly causes the underflow condition in withdrawRemainingTitn.

calling_sequence: 3. Time check passes: block.timestamp - launchTime >= 360 days (line 103-105)
5. remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0 is true (first call), so enter initialization (line 109)
10. All state changes (including steps 6-7) are rolled back
11. On next call, remainingTitnAfter1Year is still 0, loop repeats from step 5
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerable path starts at line 100, passes time checks, enters the initialization block (lines 109-115) because remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0, and then hits the unguarded subtraction at line 122. If remainingTitnAfter1Year < initialTotalClaimable, the Solidity 0.8+ checked arithmetic causes a revert. The revert rolls back the initialization, so remainingTitnAfter1Year remains 0 and the condition persists indefinitely. Meanwhile, claimTitn (line 93) is blocked after 360 days due to the >= check at line 97-98. Users have no viable claim path.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Owner deposits exactly TITN_ARB (173.7M TITN) — confirmed in arbitrumSetup.ts
No cap on TGT deposits — confirmed; no deployment script sets a cap
withdraw() is unrestricted for owner — confirmed in contract code
config_validation.deployment_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts deposits TITN_DEPOSIT_AMOUNT = 173700000000000000000000000 (173.7M TITN = TITN_ARB). No deployment script enforces a TGT deposit cap or restricts the owner's withdraw() function for TITN. The MergeTgt.ts deployment script deploys the contract with no additional guards.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Scenario 1 (Over-allocation): If total TGT supply exceeds 579M (TGT_TO_EXCHANGE), users collectively depositing all TGT during the first 90 days would push totalTitnClaimable above TITN_ARB. At 360 days, the snapshot would capture balance = TITN_ARB (minus any prior claims) and initialTotalClaimable > TITN_ARB (minus matching prior claims). The subtraction underflows permanently.

Scenario 2 (Owner withdrawal): Owner calls withdraw(titn, X) where X > 0 before the 360-day snapshot. This reduces balance without reducing totalTitnClaimable. If even 1 wei is withdrawn, balance < totalTitnClaimable at snapshot time causes the underflow.

Both scenarios result in permanent DoS of withdrawRemainingTitn. Since claimTitn is blocked at >= 360 days, all unclaimed TITN is permanently locked.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL severity. After 360 days, if the underflow condition exists, ALL users who have not fully claimed their TITN via claimTitn (before 360 days) permanently lose access to their tokens. There is no admin recovery function, no emergency withdrawal, and no way to fix the snapshot values. The contract enters a dead state for post-360-day operations. The amount at risk is up to the full TITN_ARB (173.7M TITN) if no users claimed before 360 days.
recommended_mitigations: Add a safe check before the subtraction: `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year > initialTotalClaimable ? remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable : 0;`
Enforce a cumulative cap on totalTitnClaimable in onTokenTransfer: `require(totalTitnClaimable + titnOut <= TITN_ARB, "TITN allocation exceeded");`
Restrict the withdraw() function from withdrawing TITN tokens that are allocated to users (e.g., only allow withdrawing balance above totalTitnClaimable)
Separate the initialization logic from the claim logic so the snapshot can be set without risking revert from the subsequent math
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy MergeTgt with TGT and TITN tokens. 2. Owner deposits TITN_ARB. 3. Set launchTime and lock status to OneWay. 4. Have users deposit > 579M TGT total via transferAndCall during first 90 days. 5. Verify totalTitnClaimable > TITN_ARB. 6. Advance time past 360 days. 7. Call withdrawRemainingTitn() — expect revert on underflow. 8. Call again — expect same revert (confirming perpetual DoS). 9. Call claimTitn() — expect revert (TooLateToClaimRemainingTitn). 10. Confirm funds are permanently locked.
uncertainties: The total circulating supply of TGT is not known from the provided code. If TGT's total supply is <= TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M), the over-allocation path may not be reachable in practice (though the owner withdrawal path remains viable).
Whether the owner would realistically call withdraw() on TITN before the 360-day mark is uncertain, but the code permits it.
additional_testing_needed: Verify TGT token's total supply to assess feasibility of over-allocation path
Test the exact boundary: deposit exactly TGT_TO_EXCHANGE and verify totalTitnClaimable == TITN_ARB (no underflow at boundary)
Test with owner withdrawing 1 wei of TITN before 360 days to confirm underflow occurs
Test that the revert truly rolls back the snapshot initialization (standard Solidity behavior, but worth confirming in test)
